display_name: DSCP not matching what the host set usually means remarking at the trust boundary, not a marking failure
context: Verifying end-to-end QoS and finding that the DSCP value observed on a switch differs from the value the source application set
observation: Captured or counted DSCP markings on an interface do not match the DSCP the endpoint was configured to send (often seen as DSCP 0 / best-effort)
naive_interpretation: The application is not marking traffic, or QoS marking is broken on the device
correct_interpretation: By default an access port is untrusted and the switch rewrites incoming DSCP to 0 (or to a policy-defined value) at the trust boundary. Seeing DSCP 0 or a remapped value is the trust boundary doing its job, not a marking failure. The endpoint may well be marking correctly; the switch is overriding it because the port is not configured to trust DSCP/CoS. Verify the trust state and ingress marking policy before blaming the host.
why: An untrusted port resets incoming CoS/DSCP to a default (commonly 0) so that endpoints cannot self-assign priority — this is the intended security/QoS boundary behavior
RFC 2474 defines DSCP as mutable per-hop; routers and switches are explicitly allowed to remark it according to local policy at a DiffServ boundary
Trust must be explicitly enabled (trust dscp / trust cos) or an ingress marking policy applied for host markings to survive
The observed value reflects post-classification/marking state on that device, not necessarily what arrived on the wire

confirmation_commands.purpose: Show the port trust state to see whether incoming DSCP/CoS is trusted or being reset
Reveal the ingress classification/marking policy that may be remarking DSCP at this boundary
Confirm which queue traffic landed in, which reflects the DSCP-to-queue mapping actually applied

traps: Blaming the application or NIC for unmarked traffic when the switch port is simply untrusted and zeroing DSCP
Assuming DSCP is immutable end to end — any DiffServ boundary is allowed to remark it
Checking the marking on only one hop and extrapolating to the whole path
Enabling trust globally to 'fix' the symptom, inadvertently letting endpoints inject high-priority markings

sources.title: RFC 2474 — Definition of the Differentiated Services Field (DS Field)
